title: ABC asks viewers to protest FCC attempt to "control who is allowed" on The View
gist: FCC Chairman Brendan Carr opened a proceeding that could strip The View of its longstanding 'bona fide news' exemption from equal-time rules, part of a broader pattern of FCC pressure on Disney/ABC including a review of its eight broadcast TV station licenses. The escalating regulatory campaign against a single media company has drawn bipartisan pushback.

insights.claim: FCC Chairman Brendan Carr opened a proceeding that could force The View to comply with equal-time requirements, departing from decades of FCC practice classifying daytime talk shows as bona fide news, with a July 6 comment deadline.
insights.speaker: Jon Brodkin
insights.so_what: Regulatory reclassification risk introduces a new content-control lever the FCC can apply to broadcasters, a governance overhang for Disney's media assets.
insights.locator: FCC Chairman Brendan Carr, who has repeatedly threatened

insights.claim: Carr opened an unusual review of ABC owner Disney's TV station licenses covering eight broadcast stations, ostensibly based on allegations that Disney's DEI practices violate anti-discrimination rules.
insights.speaker: Jon Brodkin
insights.so_what: A portfolio-wide license review puts the value of Disney's broadcast licenses directly at regulatory risk.
insights.locator: Carr also opened an unusual review of ABC owner Disney's

insights.claim: Disney's eight broadcast TV stations accused the FCC of trying to suppress speech in an unprecedented attack on a single company's entire portfolio of broadcast licenses.
insights.speaker: Jon Brodkin
insights.so_what: Frames the dispute as targeted enforcement, raising the stakes of the legal/regulatory fight for Disney.
insights.quote: an unprecedented attack on a single company's entire portfolio of broadcast licenses
insights.locator: The eight broadcast TV stations owned by the company protested

insights.claim: Carr began acting against The View after it aired an interview with Texas Democratic Senate candidate James Talarico, and has not opened similar proceedings into predominantly conservative talk radio shows.
insights.speaker: Jon Brodkin
insights.so_what: Asymmetric enforcement suggests political motivation, strengthening legal challenges and the perception of regulatory weaponization.
insights.locator: Carr started taking action against The View

insights.claim: Carr previously threatened the licenses of ABC stations for airing Jimmy Kimmel, and ABC said the FCC is using the license renewal process to punish a broadcaster for its editorial choices.
insights.speaker: Jon Brodkin
insights.so_what: Pattern of license threats tied to programming choices signals a repeatable pressure tactic against networks.
insights.locator: But Carr previously threatened the licenses of ABC stations

insights.claim: The View won a bona fide news exemption from the FCC via a 2002 Declaratory Ruling that ABC says remains in full force, a point Carr's public posts omitted.
insights.speaker: Jon Brodkin
insights.so_what: Existing precedent gives ABC a strong legal basis to contest reclassification, raising odds the FCC action is overturned.
insights.locator: The View itself won a bona fide news exemption

insights.claim: Carr's threats to broadcasters have drawn bipartisan opposition, with Senators Ted Cruz and Ron Wyden proposing a 'jawboning' law to stop federal officials from coercing broadcasters or tech platforms into restricting speech.
insights.speaker: Jon Brodkin
insights.so_what: Bipartisan legislative response could constrain future FCC coercion, a potential mitigant to regulatory risk for media and tech platforms.
insights.locator: Carr's threats to broadcasters like ABC have drawn bipartisan
